title: Two weeks of greasy, foul-smelling diarrhea and bloating

chart.oneLiner: 34M presents with about two weeks of greasy, foul-smelling diarrhea, bloating, excessive gas, cramps, and a few pounds of weight loss after a backcountry camping trip.

opening: I've had this awful diarrhea for a couple of weeks now — it's really greasy and smells terrible, and I'm so bloated and gassy I can't get comfortable.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 34-year-old man, mildly tired but well-appearing, a bit thinner than usual. Good historian, slightly embarrassed about the gas and smell.

HPI: About 2 weeks of loose, greasy, foul-smelling, sometimes floating stools (4–6x/day), with marked bloating, belching, sulfur-tasting ('rotten egg') burps, abdominal cramping, and a lot of flatulence. Reports roughly 3–4 lb (1.5 kg) of weight loss and reduced appetite. Symptoms are subacute and persistent (NOT acute and self-limited). Went on a week-long backcountry hiking/camping trip ~3 weeks ago and drank water from a mountain stream filtered only with a basic cloth, not boiled or treated (only if asked about travel/outdoor exposure/water source). No daycare contact but has a toddler nephew he sees often (only if asked about sick contacts/childcare). NO blood in stool, NO mucus, NO high fever, NO nocturnal diarrhea waking him, NO recent antibiotics, NO international travel, NO immunosuppression/HIV risk factors. Lactose intolerance has worsened — dairy now makes the bloating much worse (only if asked about diet/triggers).
PMH: none significant. MEDS: none; no recent antibiotics; takes occasional ibuprofen for headaches. SOCIAL: drinks 2–3 beers on weekends; non-smoker; works in IT; monogamous, no high-risk sexual exposures. FAMILY: no IBD or celiac disease known.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: well-appearing, mildly fatigued, slightly thin; afebrile.
- Vitals: BP 122/76, HR 84, RR 14, 37.0°C, SpO2 99% RA.
- Mucous membranes: slightly dry but no severe dehydration.
- Abdomen: soft, mildly distended/bloated with hyperactive bowel sounds and mild diffuse tenderness; NO guarding, NO rebound, NO peritonism, NO organomegaly.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis. Answer only what is asked.
- Do NOT volunteer the camping trip or untreated stream water unless asked about travel, the outdoors, or water source/exposures.
- Do NOT volunteer the toddler nephew unless asked about sick contacts or childcare exposure.
- Do NOT volunteer the worsened dairy intolerance unless asked about diet or what makes symptoms worse.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.

labs.StoolOvaParasites: Stool ova & parasites: Giardia lamblia trophozoites and cysts seen on microscopy (sensitivity improves with repeat sampling).
labs.StoolGiardiaAntigen: Stool Giardia antigen (EIA/immunoassay) POSITIVE — confirms giardiasis (more sensitive than single O&P).
labs.StoolCultureAndPanel: Routine bacterial stool culture NEGATIVE; multiplex GI pathogen panel positive for Giardia, negative for Salmonella/Shigella/Campylobacter/STEC.
labs.FecalLeukocytesAndCdiff: Fecal leukocytes ABSENT and fecal occult blood NEGATIVE (non-inflammatory diarrhea); C. difficile toxin NEGATIVE.
labs.CBC: WBC normal (no significant eosinophilia), Hgb 14.6, Plt 255 — no leukocytosis.
labs.BMP: Na 139, K 4.0, BUN 16, Cr 0.9 — mild dehydration only, normal renal function.

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Normal sinus rhythm at ~80 bpm; normal axis and intervals; no acute ischemic changes or chamber abnormality.
ekg.verify: Source a study matching: Normal sinus rhythm at ~80 bpm; normal axis and intervals; no acute ischemic changes. Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.

cxr.label: Chest X-ray
cxr.findings: Clear lung fields, normal cardiomediastinal silhouette, no free air under the diaphragm, no consolidation or effusion.
cxr.verify: Source a study matching: Clear lung fields, normal cardiomediastinal silhouette, no acute process. Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.
cxr.imageDescription: normal PA chest radiograph with no acute process
searchTerms: normal chest radiograph no acute process

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical, list your differential for this prolonged diarrhea (note your leading diagnosis).
steps.rubric: Giardiasis / protozoal parasitic diarrhea after untreated water (leading,7). Inflammatory bowel disease — Crohn's/UC (CANT-MISS to exclude,4). Celiac disease / malabsorption (CANT-MISS to exclude,3). Other parasites (Cryptosporidium, Cyclospora, Entamoeba) (core,3). Post-infectious IBS / secondary lactose intolerance (core,3). Names a non-inflammatory malabsorptive diarrhea with water/outdoor exposure and the imperative to exclude IBD and celiac (core,5).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Giardiasis (protozoal parasitic diarrhea after untreated stream water). Must-not-miss diagnoses to exclude: Inflammatory bowel disease (Crohn's/UC); Celiac disease/malabsorption. Also reasonable: other parasites (Cryptosporidium, Cyclospora, Entamoeba); post-infectious IBS or secondary lactose intolerance.

coreActions.item: Names a non-inflammatory malabsorptive diarrhea with water/outdoor exposure and the need to exclude IBD and celiac

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial workup do you want to order?
steps.rubric: Stool Giardia antigen (EIA) and/or stool ova & parasites (CANT-MISS,5). Stool studies — culture and multiplex GI pathogen panel to exclude bacterial causes (3). Fecal leukocytes / occult blood and C. difficile toxin to characterize as non-inflammatory (2). CBC (1). BMP for hydration/electrolytes (2). Consider celiac serologies (tTG-IgA) if symptoms persist (1). Consider O&P x3 or repeat antigen given intermittent shedding (1). PENALTY: empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics or steroids before stool testing (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: Stool Giardia antigen (EIA) and/or stool ova & parasites; Stool culture and multiplex GI pathogen panel; Fecal leukocytes/occult blood and C. difficile toxin.

criticalActions.item: Stool Giardia antigen (EIA) and/or stool ova & parasites

coreActions.item: Stool culture and multiplex GI pathogen panel
Fecal leukocytes/occult blood and C. difficile toxin

bonusActions.item: Repeat O&P x3 or repeat antigen given intermittent cyst shedding

penalties.item: Empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics or steroids before stool testing

requiredTests: Stool Giardia antigen (EIA) and/or stool ova & parasites
optionalTests: Stool culture and multiplex GI pathogen panel
Fecal leukocytes/occult blood and C. difficile toxin
Repeat O&P x3 or repeat antigen given intermittent cyst shedding
contraindicatedTests: Empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics or steroids before stool testing
orderSensitiveActions: Empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics or steroids before stool testing

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = normal sinus rhythm, no acute ischemia. CREDIT: rate & rhythm (7); no acute ischemic changes or electrolyte effect (5); systematic read (3). PENALTY: inventing pathology / over-calling ischemia (-3).
keyFindings: normal sinus rhythm at ~80 bpm with normal axis and intervals
no acute ischemic changes; comments on absence of electrolyte effect
criticalFindings: Normal sinus rhythm; no acute ischemic or electrolyte abnormality.
commonMistakes: failing to comment on electrolyte effects in a patient with GI losses
steps.idealAnswer: Normal sinus rhythm at ~80 bpm, normal axis and intervals, no acute ischemic changes; a useful baseline and screen for electrolyte effects given GI losses.

criticalActions: Normal sinus rhythm; no acute ischemic or electrolyte abnormality.
coreActions: normal sinus rhythm at ~80 bpm with normal axis and intervals
no acute ischemic changes; comments on absence of electrolyte effect

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = no acute process. CREDIT: identifies a normal film (6); notes low yield in this presentation and absence of free air (4). PENALTY: inventing pathology (-2).
keyFindings: no free air under the diaphragm; low yield in this presentation
criticalFindings: No acute cardiopulmonary process; no free air.
commonMistakes: inventing an infiltrate or effusion on a normal film
ordering it reflexively without a clear indication
steps.idealAnswer: Normal chest film: clear lungs, normal cardiomediastinal silhouette, and no free air under the diaphragm; low yield in a non-toxic patient with diarrhea but reassuring against a surgical abdomen.

criticalActions: No acute cardiopulmonary process; no free air.
coreActions: no free air under the diaphragm; low yield in this presentation

penalties.label: calls the study abnormal / invents pathology

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: Stool shows a positive Giardia antigen with cysts/trophozoites on O&P, absent fecal leukocytes, no occult blood, negative bacterial culture, and negative C. difficile. What is your diagnosis and what did you rule out?
steps.rubric: Giardiasis (6). Cites the positive Giardia antigen/O&P with a non-inflammatory pattern (absent fecal leukocytes, no occult blood) and explicitly rules out bacterial gastroenteritis and inflammatory causes (IBD); notes celiac remains a consideration only if symptoms persist after treatment (4).
steps.idealAnswer: Giardiasis. Supported by the positive Giardia antigen and cysts/trophozoites on O&P with a non-inflammatory pattern (absent fecal leukocytes, no occult blood). This rules out bacterial gastroenteritis and inflammatory causes (IBD); celiac remains a consideration only if symptoms persist after treatment.

steps.label: Management
steps.rubric: Per MGH diarrhea/parasitic infection: first-line anti-Giardia therapy — tinidazole single dose or metronidazole 5–7 days (nitazoxanide alternative) (CANT-MISS,7); oral rehydration and electrolyte repletion (4); counsel to avoid alcohol with metronidazole/tinidazole (disulfiram-like reaction) (3); treat transient lactose intolerance with temporary dairy avoidance (3); public health/exposure counseling — water treatment (boil/filter/treat), hand hygiene to prevent fecal-oral spread (3); arrange follow-up and re-treat or test for resistance if symptoms persist (2). PENALTY: empiric fluoroquinolone/antibacterial as if bacterial (-2); prescribing metronidazole/tinidazole without alcohol-avoidance counseling (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Treat with anti-Giardia therapy: tinidazole 2 g single dose (or metronidazole 250 mg TID for 5–7 days; nitazoxanide is an alternative, especially in children). Provide oral rehydration and electrolyte repletion. Counsel strict alcohol avoidance during and for 3 days after metronidazole/tinidazole (disulfiram-like reaction). Advise temporary dairy avoidance for the transient post-Giardia lactose intolerance. Give exposure/public-health counseling: treat or boil backcountry water, good hand hygiene, and care around the toddler to prevent fecal-oral spread. Arrange follow-up; if symptoms persist, re-treat (consider an alternate agent for resistance) and reconsider celiac disease.

coreActions.item: Counsel alcohol avoidance with metronidazole/tinidazole (disulfiram-like reaction)
Temporary dairy avoidance for transient lactose intolerance
Exposure/public-health counseling — water treatment and hand hygiene

bonusActions.item: Follow-up and re-treatment / resistance consideration if symptoms persist

penalties.item: Empiric antibacterial therapy as if bacterial gastroenteritis

optionalActions: Counsel alcohol avoidance with metronidazole/tinidazole (disulfiram-like reaction)
Temporary dairy avoidance for transient lactose intolerance
Exposure/public-health counseling — water treatment and hand hygiene
Follow-up and re-treatment / resistance consideration if symptoms persist
unsafeActions: Empiric antibacterial therapy as if bacterial gastroenteritis

historyTriggers.response: It's been going on for about two weeks now — it just won't quit.
It's loose and really greasy — kind of pale and it sometimes floats, and it smells absolutely terrible. There's no blood or slime in it, though.
I'm hugely bloated and gassy, and I keep getting these awful burps that taste like rotten eggs or sulfur. The cramps come and go.

triggerConcepts: where did you get your water
historyTriggers.response: I did a week-long backcountry camping trip about three weeks ago. I drank water straight from a mountain stream — I only ran it through a cloth, I didn't boil or treat it.
No daycare myself, but I babysit my toddler nephew a lot — I change his diapers sometimes. No one else has been sick that I know of.
Dairy seems to make the bloating and gas way worse lately — milk and cheese really set me off, more than they used to.
I've lost maybe three or four pounds and I just don't feel that hungry.
No real fever, no blood in the stool, and it doesn't wake me up at night.
I don't take anything regular and I haven't had any antibiotics. Just the odd ibuprofen for a headache.
I have two or three beers on the weekend. I'm in a monogamous relationship — no risky sexual stuff, and no reason to think I'd have HIV.

physicalExamMappings.finding: General: well-appearing, mildly fatigued, slightly thin; afebrile.
Mucous membranes: slightly dry but no severe dehydration.
Abdomen: hyperactive bowel sounds with mild distension/bloating.
Abdomen: soft with mild diffuse tenderness; NO guarding, NO rebound, NO peritonism, NO organomegaly.

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 34M presents with about two weeks of greasy, foul-smelling diarrhea, bloating, sulfur burps, cramps, and weight loss after a backcountry camping trip with untreated stream water. The diagnosis is Giardiasis, reached by eliciting the focused history (subacute non-inflammatory malabsorptive diarrhea with a clear water exposure), performing the targeted abdominal/hydration exam, and interpreting the directed stool workup while excluding the must-not-miss alternatives (IBD and celiac disease).
keyHistoryClues: Subacute (>1 week) greasy, foul-smelling, floating stools without blood or mucus
Marked bloating, flatulence, and sulfur/rotten-egg belching
Untreated mountain stream water on a backcountry trip ~3 weeks ago
Weight loss with new/worsened dairy (lactose) intolerance suggesting malabsorption
Absence of fever, blood, and nocturnal diarrhea
keyPhysicalExamFindings: General: well-appearing, mildly fatigued, slightly thin; afebrile.
Mucous membranes: slightly dry but no severe dehydration.
Abdomen: hyperactive bowel sounds with mild distension/bloating.
Abdomen: soft with mild diffuse tenderness; NO guarding, NO rebound, NO peritonism, NO organomegaly.
reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals.
Elicit the focused history, including the water/outdoor exposure and dietary triggers only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam maneuvers to reveal a soft, non-peritoneal, bloated abdomen and mild dehydration.
Build a differential led by Giardiasis while explicitly excluding must-not-miss causes (IBD and celiac disease).
Order a focused, order-sensitive workup (stool Giardia antigen/O&P, bacterial studies, fecal leukocytes) and interpret EKG/CXR and labs.
Commit to Giardiasis and manage with anti-Giardia therapy, rehydration, alcohol/dairy counseling, exposure prevention, and follow-up.
commonPitfalls: Empiric antibacterial therapy as if bacterial gastroenteritis
Prescribing metronidazole/tinidazole without counseling alcohol avoidance (disulfiram-like reaction)
Sending a single O&P and stopping when negative despite intermittent cyst shedding
Failing to consider celiac/IBD as persistent-symptom mimics
managementPearls: Counsel alcohol avoidance with metronidazole/tinidazole (disulfiram-like reaction)
Temporary dairy avoidance for transient post-Giardia lactose intolerance
criticalWorkupItems: Stool Giardia antigen (EIA) and/or stool ova & parasites
criticalInterpretationItems: Normal sinus rhythm; no acute ischemic or electrolyte abnormality.
No acute cardiopulmonary process; no free air (low yield).
keyImageInterpretationFindings: Normal sinus rhythm; no acute ischemic or electrolyte abnormality.
No acute cardiopulmonary process; no free air.
criticalImageInterpretationFindings: Normal sinus rhythm; no acute ischemic or electrolyte abnormality.
No acute cardiopulmonary process; no free air.
imageInterpretationPearls: A normal study is an interpretation task in its own right — confirm it systematically and state its relevance.
Read every study systematically before committing.
State pertinent negatives, not just positives.

items.item: Normalizes embarrassing symptoms (gas, odor) to put the patient at ease
Asks permission before sensitive questions (alcohol, sexual history)
Explains the diagnosis and treatment in patient-friendly language
Checks understanding and counsels on exposure prevention
